areaContext: Washington Pass scenic area, North Cascades Highway (WA-20), Blue Lakes trailhead approach
rockType: Granite and metamorphic rock with highly variable crack systems; north-facing aspects feature lichen and dampness; south-facing SW face dry and featured

firstAscent.notes: Original approach via Twisp River (16 miles); current highway access since 1972

routes.routeDescription: The classic moderate alpine rock route on Liberty Bell; 4-pitch ascent of southwest face featuring moderate cracks, faces, and slabs; excellent introduction to alpine climbing in the North Cascades

gear.notes: Finger-size cams for lower pitches; thin cracks throughout
Primary rack; hand-sized pieces most abundant
Optional; occasionally useful on pitch 2
Standard North Cascades granite; useful on pitches 1-2
Not required; crack quality consistently good for cam placement

cams.notes: Total of 6 cams standard; Beckey recommends redundancy on pitch 4 (unprotected friction slab)

nuts.notes: Less critical than cams; useful backup

cordelette.notes: One cordelette adequate for south face (featured terrain)

webbing.notes: Standard for anchor building on moderate terrain

sling_60cm.notes: Alpine standard; horn placements on upper pitches

slingRack.notes: Solid anchor placements; south face is featured and well-protected

alpineDraws.notes: Manage rope drag on 4-pitch route; wandering crack line reduces drag vs point-to-point

rope.notes: 60m standard; 50m possible with careful planning; descent via rappel from summit

ascender.notes: South-facing route typically dry by July; early season snow requires approach gear

corrections: DO NOT underestimate pitch 4—unprotected friction slab; place solid cam before entering section
DO NOT climb in wet conditions—lichen and dampness common on north aspect after rain
DO NOT skip pre-climb inspection—loose flakes present on upper pitches
Common error: trusting single cam placement on pitch 4; redundancy essential for unprotected slab
Mistake: carrying heavy rack (excess #3+); 6 cams (to #3) is complete rack
Route-finding hazard: Pitch 2 has multiple crack options; stick to main SW-trending line to avoid grade creep

gear_changes: Ultralight ice axe required for approach
early_season.conditions: Snow at base through mid-June; route itself typically dry above 6,500 ft; morning sun on SW face speeds melt
early_season.hazards: Loose rock increased from rockfall above route; snow couloir approach hazard

peak_season.conditions: Dry granite; excellent friction; minimal lichen; warm afternoon conditions ideal
peak_season.hazards: Afternoon thunderstorm risk (July-August); retreat to valley if clouds build by noon

late_season.conditions: Dry rock, perfect conditions; cooler temps; no snow approach hazard
late_season.hazards: Increasingly loose rock from freeze-thaw cycles; expect rockfall debris on approach trail

routes.routeDescription: Steeper and more engaging than Beckey Route; features excellent crack climbing, slabs, and chimneys on the north-facing aspect; prime alpine rock with good protection quality; popular for intermediate alpinists seeking Grade III experience

gear.notes: Finger cracks on lower pitches
ESSENTIAL—hand-sized cracks dominate; 4 recommended minimum
Handy on pitch 3 and summit sections
More nuts than Beckey; north face has varied crack systems
Emergency backup; rarely needed on modern ascents

cams.notes: 7 cams total; north face requires more redundancy than SW face

nuts.notes: Standard North Cascades practice; backup placements common

cordelette.notes: Two cordelettes for 5-pitch route; robust anchor building required

webbing.notes: North face is more featured; good anchor opportunities

sling_120cm.notes: Extended runners useful for pitch 4 runout

slingRack.notes: North face features solid anchor horns and solid rock

alpineDraws.notes: 5-pitch route with runouts on pitches 4-5; extended runners manage rope drag

rope.notes: 60m standard; final pitch 'slightly over 60m' per Kyle—careful belay placement or use 70m for comfort

ascender.notes: North face retains snow longer than south face; ice axe required through mid-June

corrections: DO NOT attempt Remsberg Variation accidentally on pitch 3—common mistake leading to 5.10d slab and required downclimb
DO NOT underestimate pitch 4 runout—appears easier (5.8) but protection sparse; place solid piece before committing
DO NOT bring inadequate small cams—finger-to-hand cracks require redundancy
Common error: trusting single piton placement or hollow flakes on pitch 3; test flakes before weighting
Mistake: carrying too many large cams (#4+) when #0.5-#3 covers entire route
Route-finding hazard: Remsberg Variation (5.10d slab on pitch 3) is easier to climb than correct 5.9 line; requires descent if taken

early_season.conditions: North face retains snow; frozen early morning conditions; melt by afternoon
early_season.hazards: Loose rock from freeze-thaw; rockfall hazard afternoon; snow couloir exit hazard

peak_season.conditions: Dry rock by late June; excellent friction; lichen present but manageable
peak_season.hazards: Thunderstorm risk July-August afternoon; north face remains cooler/damper than south face

late_season.conditions: Crisp fall conditions; minimal lichen; solid friction
late_season.hazards: Increasing rockfall debris from freeze-thaw; loose blocks on upper pitches

sources.verification: Detailed topo and description of NW Face variant
60+ user reviews with route-finding advice; Remsberg Variation hazard documented
Trip report with 'slightly over 60m final pitch' notation; pitch 4 runout hazard

sources.source_name: North Cascade Mountain Guides—NW Face Guide

firstAscent.notes: Featured in Fifty Classic Climbs of North America (aid version); free ascent 2016 by Blake Herrington

routes.routeDescription: The iconic 12-pitch crack climb of Liberty Bell; legendary trad route featuring sustained hand and fist cracks, off-widths, and technical slab pitches; world-class alpine rock with exposure and serious commitment; combines technical difficulty with alpine adventure

gear.notes: Finger-sized cracks; micro-cams useful
Primary hand-crack size
Larger hand and incipient off-width sections
Off-width crux pitches
Limited utility; most placements cam-only
Backup for potential aid sections (rare on free ascent)

cams.notes: Total 8 cams typical; dense protection on hand-crack pitches

webbing.notes: Multiple anchor options on big wall terrain

sling_120cm.notes: Useful for multi-piece anchors on crux pitches

slingRack.notes: Granite is featured; solid anchor placements abundant

alpineDraws.notes: 12-pitch route; extend runners to manage rope drag on crack line

rope.type: single dynamic OR half ropes (8.0-8.8mm pair recommended)
rope.notes: 70m single standard; some parties use 2x60m half ropes for full-length rappels; single 70m requires careful belay placement on several pitches

ascender.belay_device: ABD or ATC-style; assisted-braking belay device recommended for big wall
ascender.notes: No mountaineering tools for summer ascents; focus on crack climbing technique

corrections: DO NOT underestimate the 'Lithuanian Lip' crux on pitch 2 (5.13-)—world-class hard climbing; whipper potential on marginal protection
DO NOT bring inadequate micro-cams—finger cracks require #0.3-#0.75 redundancy
DO NOT assume 60m rope adequate—multiple parties report mid-pitch belays necessary on 60m; 70m strongly recommended
Common error: under-protecting crux pitches; dense cam placement standard practice
Mistake: carrying too many large cams when hand-crack route is mostly #0.75-#2 range
DO NOT descend in darkness—12-pitch rappel descent requires excellent route-finding; start descent early
Protection hazard: Several pitches feature hollow flakes; test all placements with body weight before committing

early_season.conditions: Dry rock by June; north aspect remains cool; wet rock possible after rain
early_season.hazards: Extended approach through snow; loose rock from freeze-thaw; afternoon rockfall

peak_season.conditions: Optimal conditions; dry granite; excellent friction
peak_season.hazards: High alpine thunderstorm risk afternoon (common July-August); bivouac-friendly weather

late_season.conditions: Excellent fall conditions; cool temperatures; minimal lichen
late_season.hazards: Rockfall debris increases; freeze-thaw loosens blocks

sources.source_name: American Alpine Journal (AAJ)—North Cascades Climbing Reports
sources.verification: Likely coverage of Blake Herrington FFA (2016 volume)
Contemporary ascent with detailed crux assessment and rope length notes

routes.routeDescription: Classic 3-pitch hand and crack climbing route on southwest aspect; shorter alternative to Beckey Route with sustained 5.8 hand cracks and excellent granite; popular for climbers seeking compact alpine experience with quality protection

gear.notes: ESSENTIAL—hand-crack primary geometry; 3 pieces minimum
Occasional larger sections
Backup placements; nuts less common than cams
Not required; granite quality consistently good for cams

alpineDraws.notes: Short 3-pitch route; minimal rope drag management needed

rope.notes: 50m adequate for 3-pitch route; 60m acceptable if descending via main SW face trail

ascender.crampons: Not required (south-facing; early season microspikes optional)
ascender.notes: South-facing exposure dries quickly; minimal approach hazard

corrections: DO NOT confuse pitch 1 start with Liberty Crack/Beckey Route start—three routes share initial terrain for ~150m
DO NOT under-protect hand cracks—3 #1-#2 cams minimum on each pitch
DO NOT bring large cams (#4+) unnecessarily—#1-#3 range covers entire climb
Common error: Insufficient hand-crack climbing experience; sustained 5.8 hand cracks are harder than face climbing
Mistake: Inadequate belay anchors on pitch 1-2; test placements before descending to belayer
Route-finding hazard: Multiple crack options on pitch 2; main line trends right toward false summit; easier descent contours left

early_season.conditions: South-facing; dry by mid-May; excellent friction conditions
early_season.hazards: Approach snow through early June; rockfall from higher peaks possible

peak_season.conditions: Perfect alpine rock conditions; warm afternoon temps
peak_season.hazards: Afternoon thunderstorm risk; loose rock from freeze-thaw cycles

late_season.conditions: Crisp fall weather; solid friction; minimal lichen
late_season.hazards: Increasing loose rock debris; freeze-thaw damage to upper pitches

sources.verification: Brief mention in southwest face area guide
Limited user reviews (10-15); sparse detailed beta

sources.verification: Guide service does not publish detailed Rapple Grapple guide; direct consultation recommended
Minimal documented ascents; sparse community beta

firstAscent.notes: Modern big wall route; free variation ascended 2008 by Sean Schaefer

routes.routeDescription: 11-pitch big wall route featuring sustained 5.12 bouldery terrain with three distinct crux pitches; combines technical face climbing with quality crack sections; sophisticated alpine big wall experience requiring advanced climbing skills and commitment

gear.notes: Small cams for face crux sections
Crack sections on lower and upper pitches
Occasional placement; bolts primary on modern ascents
Backup option; modern free ascents require minimal pitons

cams.notes: 5 cams typical; some pitches use bolts

cordelette.notes: 11-pitch big wall; robust anchor systems required

webbing.notes: Multiple anchor options on face terrain

sling_60cm.notes: Extended reach useful on wide feature walls

alpineDraws.notes: 11-pitch route; numerous runners needed for rope drag management

rope.type: single dynamic OR half ropes (8.0-8.8mm pair)
rope.notes: 70m single recommended; 2x60m half ropes alternative for technical rappel descent

ascender.notes: Advanced rock climbing focus; minimal mountaineering tools

corrections: DO NOT underestimate the three 5.12 bouldery crux pitches—each presents sustained hard climbing with marginal protection
DO NOT bring inadequate small cams—face climbing relies on micro and small cam placements
DO NOT assume bolt anchors—some pitches are purely trad-protected; verify current conditions
Common error: Insufficient rest strategy—11-pitch hard rock route requires energy management
Mistake: Underestimating bivouac likelihood—11 pitches may exceed daylight hours; plan headlamps and sleeping bags
Protection hazard: Bouldery crux pitches have sparse protection options; dense camming strategy required
DO NOT descend in darkness—route-finding complexity increases significantly on 11-pitch descent

peak_season.conditions: Optimal conditions; dry granite; warm for multi-day push
peak_season.hazards: Afternoon thunderstorm risk (common July-August); extended sun exposure

early_season.conditions: Long daylight hours beneficial for 11-pitch climb; possible snow on approach
early_season.hazards: Wet rock possible after rain; loose rock from freeze-thaw

late_season.conditions: Excellent rock conditions; cool temperatures reduce sweat on friction pitches
late_season.hazards: Shorter daylight hours—bivouac more likely; freeze-thaw loosens blocks

sources.verification: 20-25 user reviews; sparse contemporary ascent reports
Limited documentation; route less commonly climbed than Beckey/Liberty Crack
Modern free variation documentation
Minimal specific Thin Red Line documentation

research_metadata.methodology: 5-phase adversarial verification (Scope, Search, Fetch, Verify, Synthesize)
research_metadata.notes: Liberty Bell Mountain is the primary climbing destination in Washington Pass area (elevation 7,112 ft). Beckey Route is classic Grade II moderate route (1946); Northwest Face is Grade III alternative; Liberty Crack is world-class 12-pitch big wall featuring iconic hand crack climbing; Rapple Grapple is compact 3-pitch hand crack option; Thin Red Line is advanced 11-pitch technical big wall (5.12 bouldery). All routes on south to east-facing aspects of granite; excellent rock quality; primary hazards are loose rock (freeze-thaw cycles), thunderstorms, and exposure. Seasonal variations critical: early season (May) requires ice axe/crampons; peak season (July-August) optimal; late season (September) excellent but increasing rockfall debris.
